explicit_text: I was born in Mogadishu, Somalia in 1988, but I left when I was very young because of the civil war. I returned to Mogadishu for the first time in 2012 with my mom, and it was a mix of excitement and cultural shock. I stayed at our family's house and took many photos to capture the memories, including old family photos we had lost. I went back again in 2013 with my father, who hadn't been back since 1991, and it was special to see him reconnect with his hometown and old friends.

I went back again in 2013 with my father, who hadn't been back since 1991, and it was special to see him reconnect with his hometown and old friends.

explicit_text: Somalia is my birthplace, but I left in 1991 due to the Somali Civil War. Growing up away from Somalia, I always dreamed of returning, and finally, in 2012, I went back to reconnect with my roots. Through multiple visits and an internship with a research institute as part of my graduate program, I gained a deeper understanding of Somali society and culture. These experiences helped me develop a stronger sense of identity and belonging.

These experiences helped me develop a stronger sense of identity and belonging.

explicit_text: After leaving Somalia in 1991 because of the civil war, I lived in Kenya with my grandmother for many years. Specifically, I stayed in Nairobi, which became my home during that time. Kenya was a place of refuge and safety for me before I eventually moved to Minnesota in 2000. Living there shaped part of my early life away from Somalia.

explicit_text: Nairobi, Kenya, was where I lived with my grandmother after fleeing Somalia in 1991. It was my home for several years before I moved to Minnesota in 2000. Nairobi provided a safe environment during a turbulent time in my life, and it was an important chapter between my early years in Somalia and my new life in the United States.

Minnesota is where I grew up as a young adult and where I pursued my education, including my graduate program. It was from Minnesota that I planned and made my trips back to Somalia, reconnecting with my heritage and family.

explicit_text: The Somali Civil War forced my family to leave Somalia in 1991 when I was just a child. This conflict changed the course of my life, leading us to seek safety in Kenya and eventually the United States. The war's impact made me long to understand my homeland better, which motivated my visits back to Somalia and my research internship there years later.

explicit_text: During my 2013 trip to Mogadishu with my father, I used my Canon camera to capture important moments, including my dad reuniting with his best friend after over twenty years. The Canon camera helped me take epic photos of the city and family, despite some locals being uncomfortable with photography. It was an essential tool for documenting my experiences and preserving memories during that special visit.

It was an essential tool for documenting my experiences and preserving memories during that special visit.

explicit_text: On a later trip to Somalia for my graduate internship, I used my small pink iPad, an Apple device, to take pictures and document my experience. This technology made it easier to capture everyday life and research activities, reflecting how photography has become part of our lives globally. Using the iPad helped me connect with Somalia in a new way and deepen my understanding of the country and myself.
